draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer low-light interfaces that reduce visual fatigue during early morning/evening runs
facade.label: Minimalist dark mode
facade.acceptImplies: Explore dark palettes, high contrast typography, reduced visual hierarchy for glanceable data
facade.rejectImplies: Test lighter backgrounds, colorful accents, or higher visual density to compete for attention

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer a friendly, approachable voice over clinical precision
facade.label: Cheerful and casual
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use conversational language, upbeat messaging, and a personality that feels like a running buddy rather than a fitness coach
facade.rejectImplies: The app should adopt a more formal, data-driven, authoritative tone focused on performance metrics and objective information

evidence.hypothesis: Runners prefer low-light interfaces that reduce visual fatigue during early morning/evening runs
evidence.implication: Explore dark palettes, high contrast typography, reduced visual hierarchy for glanceable data

facade.hypothesis: Runners expect gesture-based interactions (swipe, shake) over tap buttons for quick weather checks mid-run
facade.label: Swipe up to refresh
facade.acceptImplies: Build gestural controls throughout (swipe for hourly, shake for alerts, drag for route overlay)
facade.rejectImplies: Stick with tap-based UI; prioritize clarity and discoverability over physical gestures

facade.hypothesis: Users want encouragement and positive reinforcement while running, preferring warm, uplifting visuals over clinical data displays
facade.label: Glowing warmth
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use warm color palettes (ambers, oranges, soft golds), gentle gradients, and encouraging language. Data should feel supportive rather than judgmental.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer cooler, more neutral, or clinical aesthetics. The app should emphasize clarity and objectivity over emotional warmth or motivational tone.

facade.hypothesis: Runners want effort levels visually encoded (not raw numbers) to make splits intuitive at a glance
facade.label: Pace zones with color
facade.acceptImplies: User values quick visual interpretation of running intensity over exact pace readouts; favors zones/bands over point data
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers literal numbers and precision; distrusts abstraction; wants to see exact splits and paces always visible

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance widgets on home screen vs. navigating into detailed forecast views
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. neutral, factual weather reporting
Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented vibe during runs
Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV index)
Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback when checking conditions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance widgets on home screen vs. navigating into detailed forecast views
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. neutral, factual weather reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented vibe during runs
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV index)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback when checking conditions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners value quick access to current metrics (pace, distance, HR) alongside weather, suggesting a persistent left sidebar for vital stats beats full-screen weather cards
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure: sidebar persistence vs modal/card-based disclosure
facade.label: Sidebar with run stats
facade.acceptImplies: Runner priorities lean toward data density + quick glance access; persistent navigation structure will likely dominate other layout choices
facade.rejectImplies: Runners prefer focused, single-task screens; weather info should be primary with stats as secondary overlay or tab; card-based or full-bleed approach more appropriate

facade.hypothesis: Runners need high-visibility, high-contrast interfaces that feel motivating and match the intensity of physical exertion, especially in daylight conditions
facade.label: Bright and energetic
facade.acceptImplies: Explore vibrant color schemes, high saturation, sunny/daylight-optimized designs that energize rather than calm
facade.rejectImplies: Dark mode hypothesis strengthens; runners may prioritize eye comfort over motivational intensity

draft.title: RunWeather – Runner's Forecast Companion
draft.summary: A weather app designed specifically for runners, showing real-time conditions, air quality, and run-friendly metrics like wind speed and temperature feel. Clean, minimal interface focused on actionable data for outdoor training.
draft.html:
RunWeather

Your running forecast

San Francisco, CA
72°
72°F
Partly Cloudy
Feels Like
68°F
Wind
8 mph
Humidity
62%
Air Quality
Good

Run Readiness

Temperature Ideal
Wind Conditions Good
Air Quality Excellent
Precipitation Risk Low
